summary: Spolsky contrasts two management cultures: Microsoft's hands-off approach where employees owned their areas and resolved conflicts themselves, versus Juno's 'Command and Conquer' style where managers micromanaged decisions they were least qualified to make. Despite Juno hiring brilliant people, its vertical hierarchy and hit-and-run management drove talented employees away because they had no autonomy or upward mobility. Spolsky argues that command-and-control management wastes the talent a company worked hard to hire and makes smart people miserable. He concludes by outlining his own company's culture: flat management, individual ownership, and decisions made by the person with the most information.
key_points: Hiring smart people is necessary but insufficient — you must also give them autonomy and ownership over their work
Command-and-control management causes the person with the least information to make decisions, producing worse outcomes
Microsoft managers refused to resolve conflicts between reports, forcing the people with the most context to work it out themselves
Juno's 'hit and run management' — executives dropping in to give orders without understanding context — created a culture of frustration and attrition
A flat organizational structure where everyone owns their area produces both better decisions and happier employees
money_quotes: If you hire all A people, they'll also hire A people. But if you hire B people, they'll hire the C people and then it's all over.
Who's the person who knows least about the problem? Obviously, it's the manager — who was just hauled in at the last minute for Conflict Resolution.
At Microsoft, the manager would usually refuse to make the decision. After all, they have the least information about the problem.
Nobody at Juno owned anything, they just worked on it, and different layers of management happily stuck their finger into every pie.
Hit and run management is but one symptom of what I would call Command and Control Management… something right out of the General Motors 1953 operations manual.

It's completely inappropriate because it makes people unhappy, it causes the person with the least information to make the decisions, and it doesn't allow a corporation to take advantage of all the talents of the people it hired.
When is Command and Conquer management acceptable? Well, it might work where your company is a Herd of Coconuts — a bunch of underqualified morons who need herding.
key_insight: Hiring brilliant people and then micromanaging them is worse than not hiring them at all — great talent requires ownership and autonomy to produce great results.
